chunk_summary: This section discusses BMI-for-age classification for diagnosing thinness in children aged 5–19, differential diagnosis, and linking to government programs like PMPOSHAN, RKSK, and WIFS for management and follow-up.
learning_objectives: Understand BMI-for-age thresholds for diagnosing thinness and severe thinness in children aged 5–19 years.
Identify clinical and psychosocial factors contributing to thinness in this age group.
Learn management strategies, including dietary plans and program linkages, for moderate acute malnutrition.
Recognize the importance of follow-up using weight and BMI trends.
key_facts: Thinness is defined as BMI-for-age (BAZ) below -2, and severe thinness as BAZ below -3.
Always interpret BMI-for-age alongside height-for-age to account for stunting.
Urgent escalation is required for danger signs like rapid weight loss, dehydration, or severe illness.
Management includes three meals, two nutritious snacks, daily protein, and fat add-ons.
Government programs like PMPOSHAN (school meals), RKSK (adolescent health), and WIFS (anemia prevention) support intake and monitoring.
Follow-up should document weight and BMI trends rather than rely on single measurements.
reference_quote: Thinness is body mass index-for-age (BAZ) below -2, and severe thinness below -3. Always interpret BAZ together with height-for-age because a short child may appear 'less thin' by BMI even with significant undernutrition.
document_key_topics: Differential diagnosis of thinness in ages 5–19
